item.title: Sorry incels, but women are better investors — 44 replies — 84.2/hr
item.content:encoded:
Sorry incels, but women are better investors

Open thread • Replies: 44 • Updated: 2026-04-02 15:19 UTC

OP

New research confirms a superior performance by female traders

Women have had a pretty bad reputation in global financial markets. As investors, the perception is that they joined the game too late, they saved too little and they invested too conservatively.

But a growing number of surveys and research papers suggest this is a stereotype that is out of date. The reality is that women actually spend more time researching their options than men, are better at matching their investments to their life goals, tend to trade less and when they do, remain calmer during the storms that unnerve male investors on the financial high seas.

So, recent publicity around an NAB report suggesting that women are losing large sums of money because they are too conservative in their investment choices, has raised hackles. Not least because it claims there are anatomical differences in the brains of men and women that influence investment decisions.

One of the most in-depth pieces of research to support the view that women make better investors has come from UNSW Business School

The road the academics travelled was a long one, encompassing a 17-year period, and focused on Finland due to the transparency of its financial marketplace.

The study matched daily trade by male and female individual investors in Nokia and 27 other major Finnish stocks and found that there was a continuous female compounded rate of return of 21.44% per annum, rising to 43.16% per annum just for the main stock, Nokia.

Women individual investors also outperformed domestic and international institutional investors with an even more substantial gain of €1,654.24 million.

item.title: $300 oil? — 4 replies — 16.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
$300 oil?

Open thread • Replies: 4 • Updated: 2026-04-02 15:50 UTC

OP

Just asked Google, what's what they told me.

>To bring the gold-to-oil ratio back down to the historical average of 16, assuming the price of gold remains exactly where it is today ($4,650), the price of oil would need to increase significantly.

Here is the math:

Current Gold Price: $4,650

Target Ratio: 16

Calculation: $4,650 ÷ 16 = $290.63

WTI Crude oil would need to reach roughly $291 per barrel for the ratio to normalize at 16. Given that oil is currently trading around $108, this means the price of crude would need to nearly triple.

Alternatively, if oil prices remained flat at $108, the price of gold would have to crash to about $1,728 per ounce ($108 x 16) to reach that same historical ratio.

The Zone is a very complicated system of traps, and they're all deadly. I don't know what goes on here when people are absent, but the moment someone shows up, everything comes into motion. Old traps disappear and new ones emerge. Safe spots become impassable. One minute your path is easy, the next it's hopelessly convoluted. That's the Zone. It may seem capricious, but it is what we've made it with our condition. I've seen people stop halfway and go back. Some of them even died on the very threshold of the Room. But everything that happens here depends not on the Zone, but on us!
>So it lets the good ones pass and kills the bad ones?
I don't know... I think it lets those pass who have lost all hope. Not good or bad, but miserable people. But even the most miserable will die if they don't know how to behave. You were lucky. It only gave you a warning.
